**Regions of the Czech Republic** ([Czech](/wiki/Czech_language "Czech language"): *kraje* [[ˈkrajɛ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Czech "Help:IPA/Czech"); singular *kraj* [[kraj]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Czech "Help:IPA/Czech") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Cs-kraj.ogg "File:Cs-kraj.ogg")) are higher-level territorial self-governing units of the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_the_Czech_Republic&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Czech_Republic_-_Bohemia%2C_Moravia_and_Silesia_%28en%29.png/330px-Czech_Republic_-_Bohemia%2C_Moravia_and_Silesia_%28en%29.png)](/wiki/File:Czech_Republic_-_Bohemia,_Moravia_and_Silesia_(en).png)

Historical lands and current administrative regions

See also: [History of administrative divisions of Czechoslovakia](/wiki/History_of_administrative_divisions_of_Czechoslovakia "History of administrative divisions of Czechoslovakia")

The first regions (*[kraje](/wiki/Kraj "Kraj")*) were created in the [Kingdom of Bohemia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bohemia "Kingdom of Bohemia") in the 14th century. At the beginning of the 15th century, Bohemia was already divided into 12 regions, but their borders were not fixed due to the frequent changes in the borders of the estates. During the reign of [George of Poděbrady](/wiki/George_of_Pod%C4%9Bbrady "George of Poděbrady") (1458–1471), Bohemia was divided into 14 regions, which remained so until 1714, when their number was reduced to 12 again. From 1751 to 1850, after the four largest regions were divided, the kingdom consisted of 16 regions. Between 1850 and 1862, there were several reforms and the number of regions fluctuated between 7 and 13. Due to the parallel establishment of political districts in 1848, however, their importance declined. In 1862, the regions were abolished, although the regional authorities had some powers until 1868.[[1]](#cite_note-mvcr-1)

[Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia") was divided into four regions in 1529. In 1569–1735, their number was five, then the number increased to six. After the introduction of the political districts in 1850, Moravia consisted of two regions. In 1855–1860, there were six regions, but in 1860 they were abolished.[[1]](#cite_note-mvcr-1)

[Czech Silesia](/wiki/Czech_Silesia "Czech Silesia") was not divided into regions until 1783, when it was divided into two regions. From 1850, Czech Silesia formed one region.[[1]](#cite_note-mvcr-1)

From the 1860s to 1948, the [Czech lands](/wiki/Czech_lands "Czech lands") were divided into counties and districts. Regions were reintroduced in 1949 in [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"). From 1949 to 1960, the Czech part of Czechoslovakia was divided into the Capital City of Prague and 13 regions.[[2]](#cite_note-2) In 1960–1999, the Czech part of Czechoslovakia was divided into the Capital City of Prague and following 7 regions:[[3]](#cite_note-3)

* Central Bohemian Region (*Středočeský kraj*) with the capital in [Prague](/wiki/Prague "Prague")
* South Bohemian Region (*Jihočeský kraj*) with the capital in [České Budějovice](/wiki/%C4%8Cesk%C3%A9_Bud%C4%9Bjovice "České Budějovice")
* West Bohemian Region (*Západočeský kraj*) with the capital in [Plzeň](/wiki/Plze%C5%88 "Plzeň")
* North Bohemian Region (*Severočeský kraj*) with the capital in [Ústí nad Labem](/wiki/%C3%9Ast%C3%AD_nad_Labem "Ústí nad Labem")
* East Bohemian Region (*Východočeský kraj*) with the capital in [Hradec Králové](/wiki/Hradec_Kr%C3%A1lov%C3%A9 "Hradec Králové")
* South Moravian Region (*Jihomoravský kraj*) with the capital in [Brno](/wiki/Brno "Brno")
* North Moravian Region (*Severomoravský kraj*) with the capital in [Ostrava](/wiki/Ostrava "Ostrava")

According to the Act no. 129/2000 Coll. ("Law on Regions"), which implements Chapter VII of the [Czech Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitution of the Czech Republic"), the Czech Republic is divided into thirteen regions and one [capital city](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") with regional status as of 1 January 2000.[[4]](#cite_note-law-4) The capital city of Prague, which has simultaneously the status of a region and a [municipality](/wiki/Obec "Obec"), is treated by Act no. 131/2000 Coll. ("Law on Capital City of Prague").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Competences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_the_Czech_Republic&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Competences")]

Rights and obligations of the regions include:[[4]](#cite_note-law-4)

* Establishment of secondary schools;
* Responsibility for hospitals and social facilities;
* Construction and repair of second and third class roads;
* Organization of integrated transport systems;
* Ordering of public intermunicipal transport;
* Protection of the nature;
* Cooperation in the distribution of EU funds within the [NUTS-2](/wiki/NUTS_statistical_regions_of_the_Czech_Republic "NUTS statistical regions of the Czech Republic") regions;
* Tasks within the integrated rescue system;
* Right to propose laws to the [Chamber of Deputies](/wiki/Chamber_of_Deputies_of_the_Czech_Republic "Chamber of Deputies of the Czech Republic") and submit complaints to the [Constitutional Court](/wiki/Constitutional_Court_of_the_Czech_Republic "Constitutional Court of the Czech Republic").

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_the_Czech_Republic&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Government")]

Every region is governed by a regional council, headed by a governor (*[hejtman](/wiki/Hejtman "Hejtman")*). Elections to regional councils take place every four years. The number of council members varies according to the population in the region and consists of 45 (population under 600,000), 55 (population 600,000–900,000) or 65 people (population over 900,000).[[4]](#cite_note-law-4)
